DPDP_Control_Description: The DPDP Policy shall formally define 'Personal Data' in accordance with the applicable provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, establishing a clear understanding of the term for consistent application across all data processing activities.  (In accordance with Section 2(t) of the DPDP Act, 2023, “Personal Data” is defined as any information related to an individual that, either directly or indirectly, can be used to identify that person. This includes—but is not limited to—identifiers such as name, identification numbers, location data, online identifiers, or any factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural, or social identity of an individual. All data assets that satisfy this definition must be treated as Personal Data and handled in a manner that ensures compliance with statutory requirements.)
Relationship_Path: Section 1 -> NO GDPR MAPPING

DPDP_Control_Description: The Company’s DPDP Privacy Policy and associated Data Processing Agreements with relevant vendors are established to ensure that the collection and processing of personal data is conducted in a fair, lawful, and transparent manner, in alignment with the principles outlined in the Digital Personal Data Protection Act.
GDPR_Control_Description: The controller (fiduciary) shall inform the data subject (Principal) without delay of the reasons for not taking action on the data subject (principal)request.

DPDP_Control_Description: Organizations must ensure that any transfer of personal data outside the territory of India complies strictly with the conditions prescribed under DPDPA 2023. Personal data may only be transferred to jurisdictions approved by the Central Government, which will maintain a whitelist of permissible countries. Transfers must be undertaken with appropriate safeguards to ensure that the data remains subject to a level of protection comparable to DPDPA standards. Organizations must evaluate legal, regulatory, and contractual mechanisms to protect transferred data, such as entering into binding contractual clauses, conducting data protection impact assessments, and ensuring that data subjects’ rights are preserved in cross-border contexts. Data Principals must be informed about the possibility of cross-border transfers, and explicit consent must be obtained if required. Any transfer-related risks must be documented, and transfer agreements should mandate breach notifications, cooperation obligations, and compliance with grievance redressal mechanisms.
GDPR_Control_Description: Data processing follows applicable compliance requirements.
Relationship_Path: Section 3 -> Rule 14.0 -> DPDP-31 -> GDPR-36 -> Art.36 -> Third Party Sharing/Collection

DPDP_Control_Description: The organization shall establish and maintain a comprehensive breach notification process that ensures timely detection, assessment, and reporting of personal data breaches. Upon breach identification, an initial assessment must be completed within 24 hours. Qualifying breaches must be reported to the Data Protection Board within 72 hours, with notifications including the breach nature, data categories affected, number of impacted Data Principals, potential consequences, and remedial actions. When breaches are likely to cause significant harm, affected Data Principals must be notified within 7 days using clear language that explains the breach, its impact, protective recommendations, and organizational response measures. The organization shall maintain a detailed breach register documenting all incidents for five years. Third-party vendors must report breaches within 24 hours of discovery. To ensure readiness, quarterly staff training and biannual simulation exercises shall be conducted. Regular audits must verify control effectiveness and DPDPA compliance.
Relationship_Path: Section 7 -> NO GDPR MAPPING

DPDP_Section_Name: Processing of personal data outside India.
DPDP_Control_Description: Organizations must ensure that any transfer of personal data outside the territory of India complies strictly with the conditions prescribed under DPDPA 2023. Personal data may only be transferred to jurisdictions approved by the Central Government, which will maintain a whitelist of permissible countries. Transfers must be undertaken with appropriate safeguards to ensure that the data remains subject to a level of protection comparable to DPDPA standards. Organizations must evaluate legal, regulatory, and contractual mechanisms to protect transferred data, such as entering into binding contractual clauses, conducting data protection impact assessments, and ensuring that data subjects’ rights are preserved in cross-border contexts. Data Principals must be informed about the possibility of cross-border transfers, and explicit consent must be obtained if required. Any transfer-related risks must be documented, and transfer agreements should mandate breach notifications, cooperation obligations, and compliance with grievance redressal mechanisms.
GDPR_Control_Description: Data processing follows applicable compliance requirements.
Relationship_Path: Section 16 -> Rule 14.0 -> DPDP-31 -> GDPR-36 -> Art.36 -> Third Party Sharing/Collection

DPDP_Control_Description:
The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDPA) grants specific exemptions to the processing of personal data when it is necessary for journalistic purposes. These exemptions aim to balance two critical interests — safeguarding individual privacy and preserving the freedom of speech and expression, particularly when serving the public interest. Under these provisions, certain compliance requirements such as obtaining explicit consent, strict purpose limitation, and data storage restrictions may not apply if personal data is processed solely for journalistic activities, including news reporting, editorial writing, and investigative journalism.

However, the exemption is not automatic. It applies only if:

The data processing is genuinely necessary for journalistic activities.

The activity serves a public interest purpose (e.g., informing society on matters of significance).

The rights and freedoms of the individuals concerned are reasonably respected (i.e., no unjustified harm, defamation, or data misuse).

Journalistic entities must still act responsibly, ensuring fairness, accuracy, and accountability. Misuse of the exemption — such as processing personal data without a clear journalistic need — can lead to regulatory scrutiny and penalties.
Relationship_Path: Section 17 -> NO GDPR MAPPING

DPDP_Control_Description: The organization shall establish and maintain a grievance redressal mechanism that allows Data Principals to file complaints regarding the handling of their personal data. Complaints must be addressed promptly, with an option for escalation to the Data Protection Board (DPB) if unresolved.
Relationship_Path: Section 18 -> NO GDPR MAPPING

DPDP_Section_Name: Composition and qualifications for appointment of Chairperson and Members.
DPDP_Control_Description: The organization shall establish and maintain a comprehensive breach notification process that ensures timely detection, assessment, and reporting of personal data breaches. Upon breach identification, an initial assessment must be completed within 24 hours. Qualifying breaches must be reported to the Data Protection Board within 72 hours, with notifications including the breach nature, data categories affected, number of impacted Data Principals, potential consequences, and remedial actions. When breaches are likely to cause significant harm, affected Data Principals must be notified within 7 days using clear language that explains the breach, its impact, protective recommendations, and organizational response measures. The organization shall maintain a detailed breach register documenting all incidents for five years. Third-party vendors must report breaches within 24 hours of discovery. To ensure readiness, quarterly staff training and biannual simulation exercises shall be conducted. Regular audits must verify control effectiveness and DPDPA compliance.
GDPR_Control_Description: In the case of a personal data breach, data controllers notify supervisory authorities within 72 hours.
Relationship_Path: Section 19 -> Rule 16.0 -> DPDP-19 -> GDPR-25 -> Art.25 -> Third Party Sharing/Collection

DPDP_Section_Name: Salary, allowances payable to and term of office.
DPDP_Control_Description:
The organization may process employee personal data without explicit consent, as permitted under DPDP Act exemptions, for essential HR functions. These functions include recruitment, onboarding, payroll administration, benefits management, performance evaluations, and statutory compliance. Data processing must strictly adhere to the principle of data minimization, ensuring that only relevant data is collected and used for specified purposes.

For organizations offering HR, recruitment, and payroll services to clients, clear and documented agreements must define data handling responsibilities, security measures, and compliance with applicable data protection laws. Employee data processing must be protected with robust technical and organizational safeguards, including access controls, encryption of sensitive data, and regular security assessments. Cross-border transfers of employee data must comply with DPDP Act and the applicable international data protection regulations in the destination jurisdiction, ensuring secure data handling across borders.

Furthermore, organizations must maintain accurate and comprehensive records of all employee data processing activities and perform regular compliance reviews to ensure continued alignment with DPDP Act requirements and industry standards.

DPDP_Control_Description:
The organization may process employee personal data without explicit consent, as permitted under DPDP Act exemptions, for essential HR functions. These functions include recruitment, onboarding, payroll administration, benefits management, performance evaluations, and statutory compliance. Data processing must strictly adhere to the principle of data minimization, ensuring that only relevant data is collected and used for specified purposes.

For organizations offering HR, recruitment, and payroll services to clients, clear and documented agreements must define data handling responsibilities, security measures, and compliance with applicable data protection laws. DPDP_Section_Name: Procedure to be followed by Board.
DPDP_Control_Description: Organizations must establish a robust data protection compliance framework to avoid regulatory penalties as prescribed under the DPDPA 2023. The Act empowers the Data Protection Board (DPB) to impose financial penalties of up to ₹250 crores per violation, depending on the severity, nature, and impact of the non-compliance. Factors such as the volume and sensitivity of personal data involved, repeated non-compliance, failure to mitigate harm, and cooperation during investigations will influence the amount of penalty levied. To minimize penalty risk, organizations must maintain documented evidence of compliance with all applicable requirements, including consent management, breach notification, security safeguards, grievance handling, and children's data protection. Internal mechanisms must be established to detect, report, and remediate violations promptly. Senior management should be made accountable for overseeing compliance efforts and responding to any regulatory inquiries or investigations.
GDPR_Control_Description: Communications regarding data breaches must follow proper processes including describing the nature of the breach.

DPDP_Control_Description: The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDPA) grants both organizations and individuals the right to appeal any decision, direction, or order issued by the Data Protection Board (DPB). Appeals must be filed before the designated Appellate Tribunal established under the Act. The right to appeal ensures due process and provides a structured legal remedy for parties aggrieved by the Board’s rulings. The appeal must typically be filed within 60 days from the date of receipt of the DPB’s order. The Tribunal is empowered to review the DPB’s findings, examine the grounds of appeal, and either uphold, modify, or set aside the Board’s decision. Organizations must ensure they maintain detailed documentation of all DPB interactions, responses, and compliance actions to support any appeal processes if necessary.
Relationship_Path: Section 29 -> NO GDPR MAPPING

DPDP_Section_Name: Orders passed by Appellate Tribunal to be executable as decree.
DPDP_Control_Description: Organizations must establish a structured and efficient grievance redressal mechanism to address complaints and concerns raised by Data Principals. The mechanism must provide Data Principals with an accessible and transparent process to submit grievances related to the processing of their personal data. Complaints must be acknowledged promptly and resolved within a reasonable time, not exceeding the timelines prescribed by regulatory requirements. A dedicated Grievance Officer must be appointed, whose contact details must be published in the organization's privacy notices. The Grievance Officer must ensure impartial investigation, provide updates to the complainant, and document the resolution process. In case a grievance remains unresolved or inadequately addressed, Data Principals must be informed of their right to escalate the complaint to the Data Protection Board (DPB). Records of grievances, investigations, and resolutions must be securely maintained for audit and compliance purposes.
Relationship_Path: Section 30 -> NO GDPR MAPPING

DPDP_Section_Name: Crediting sums realised by way of penalties to Consolidated Fund of India.
DPDP_Control_Description: Organizations are subject to a structured penalty framework for violations of data protection obligations. The Act specifies a range of financial penalties depending on the nature, severity, and impact of the violation. Penalties can range from ₹50 lakh for less severe breaches, such as failure to fulfill grievance redressal obligations, to up to ₹250 crores for serious infractions, including significant data breaches, failure to implement reasonable security safeguards, or non-compliance with lawful directions issued by the Data Protection Board. The Board has discretion to determine the final amount based on factors like the nature of personal data affected, duration of violation, harm caused to individuals, and the degree of cooperation during investigations. Organizations must adopt a risk-based compliance strategy to minimize exposure to regulatory fines and maintain a proactive incident management and reporting process.

DPDP_Section_Name: Power of Central Government to issue directions.
DPDP_Control_Description: Under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDPA), personal data can be processed without obtaining fresh consent during mergers, acquisitions, or similar corporate restructuring events, provided that adequate safeguards are implemented to protect the data. This exemption allows the seamless transfer of business assets, including personal data, when there is a lawful basis for the corporate transaction. However, organizations must ensure that the processing is limited strictly to purposes related to the merger or acquisition and that personal data is not misused or disclosed beyond what is necessary for the transaction. Safeguards may include confidentiality agreements, data protection clauses in transaction documents, and access restrictions to sensitive information. Post-transaction, the successor entity must continue to honor the data protection obligations under DPDPA, including respecting data principal rights and ensuring data security.
Relationship_Path: Section 37 -> NO GDPR MAPPING

DPDP_Control_Description: Personal data may be processed without obtaining consent during public health emergencies, epidemics, pandemics, or other disasters that pose a risk to public safety or health. The scope of processing must be limited strictly to purposes necessary for managing the emergency, such as disease control, healthcare delivery, or disaster response. Entities must ensure appropriate security measures are in place to safeguard the data during use. Once the emergency concludes, continued use or storage of the data must comply with standard data protection obligations, including deletion if the data is no longer required.

DPDP_Section_Name: Laying of rules and certain notifications.
DPDP_Control_Description: Organizations must ensure that any transfer of personal data outside the territory of India complies strictly with the conditions prescribed under DPDPA 2023. Personal data may only be transferred to jurisdictions approved by the Central Government, which will maintain a whitelist of permissible countries. Transfers must be undertaken with appropriate safeguards to ensure that the data remains subject to a level of protection comparable to DPDPA standards. Organizations must evaluate legal, regulatory, and contractual mechanisms to protect transferred data, such as entering into binding contractual clauses, conducting data protection impact assessments, and ensuring that data subjects’ rights are preserved in cross-border contexts. Data Principals must be informed about the possibility of cross-border transfers, and explicit consent must be obtained if required. Any transfer-related risks must be documented, and transfer agreements should mandate breach notifications, cooperation obligations, and compliance with grievance redressal mechanisms.
